summary: Gruber tests iPhone 4's 3G data performance against the iPhone 3GS and iPad 3G, prompted by Andy Ihnatko's review highlighting improved HSDPA and new HSUPA support. Using the FCC's broadband test app, he finds the iPhone 4 delivers roughly double the download speed and ten times the upload speed of the 3GS, with dramatically better latency. He also tests the antenna-gate scenario, finding that holding the phone in his left hand over the antenna gap reduces download speed by about a third — but even degraded, it still outperforms the 3GS. The iPad 3G performs only marginally better than the 3GS.
key_points: iPhone 4 download speeds are roughly twice as fast as the iPhone 3GS on AT&T's 3G network
Upload speeds see the most dramatic improvement — over ten times faster than the 3GS thanks to new HSUPA support
Latency improves by roughly an order of magnitude compared to both the 3GS and iPad 3G
Even with the antenna attenuation from holding the phone over the gap, the iPhone 4 still outperforms the 3GS in downloads
The iPad 3G's cellular performance is only marginally better than the older iPhone 3GS
money_quotes: So I'm seeing download speeds twice as fast as on an iPhone 3GS, and upload speeds over ten times faster.
Latency is about an order of magnitude better as well.
Holding the iPhone 4 in my hand drops the 3G download speed by about a third, but it's still faster than the 3GS.
Upload speed and latency didn't seem affected by holding it in my hand.
The iPad doesn't fare much better than the 3GS.
key_insight: The iPhone 4's cellular radio improvements are so substantial that even with the antenna-gate signal degradation, it still delivers faster data speeds than its predecessor.
